summary: This cluster focuses on the brown alga *Ecklonia cava*, investigating its bioactive potential through fermentation and enzyme-assisted extraction (EAE) to produce anti-inflammatory polysaccharides. Researchers employed microorganisms such as *Lactobacillus brevis*, *Candida utilis*, and *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* to ferment the algal biomass, which enhanced carbohydrate and protein yields compared to unfermented controls. A key finding was that the isolated polysaccharide fraction, specifically those greater than 30 kDa, significantly inhibited nitric oxide (NO) production in LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophage cells, demonstrating potent anti-inflammatory activity. The geographic scope is centered on marine resources from East Asia, where *E. cava* is commonly harvested, with the methodological emphasis on optimizing extraction processes to isolate high-value bioactive compounds.
